planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is reliably warm; in frost-free zones 9-12 seed can be sown at any time during the warm season.

directions: Vigorous tropical perennial herb or shrub in the aster family with opposite, triangular leaves and dense clusters of white to pale lavender fluffy composite flowers from fall through winter, popular with monarch butterflies and small native bees. Native to tropical America and widely naturalized in open disturbed habitats throughout the tropics and subtropical US, it grows extremely fast in warm, humid climates and is considered invasive in parts of Africa and Asia. In frost-free zones 9-12 it makes an effective naturalistic backdrop or wildlife garden plant but requires containment in smaller gardens. Plant in full sun in average to moderately moist soil and cut back hard after flowering to maintain a manageable shrubby form.
sources: University of Florida IFAS: Chromolaena odorata

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow cold stratification over winter for improved spring germination.

directions: A highly unusual native sunflower of the southeastern coastal plain entirely lacking ray flowers, bearing only chocolate-brown disk heads on stiff, upright stems in summer and fall. Native to wet flatwoods, longleaf pine savannas, and bog margins from North Carolina to Florida and Mississippi, it thrives in moist to wet, sandy, acidic soil in full sun. Despite lacking petals, the dark disk heads attract specialist native bees and provide seed for songbirds through fall and winter. An unusual and conversation-worthy specimen for native savanna gardens and rain gardens in zones 7-9, it pairs well with other wet-flatwoods natives such as Liatris and native sedges.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society: Helianthus radula

planting.note: Direct sow in fall on moist, acidic, sandy soil; cold stratification over winter improves germination rates.

directions: Native perennial sunflower of wet pine flatwoods and longleaf pine savannas of the southeastern coastal plain, producing bright yellow daisy-like flowers 2-3 inches across in late summer and fall on tall, upright stems. The narrow, rough basal leaves differ markedly in shape from the smaller stem leaves, giving the plant its common name. Grows best in moist to wet, sandy, acidic soil in full sun but tolerates seasonal standing water; plant in mass for the most striking late-season display. Seeds attract finches and sparrows, making it an underused native sunflower for wet, full-sun pollinator and bird gardens in the Gulf Coast region.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox: Helianthus heterophyllus

planting.note: Direct sow in fall on moist, acidic soil; seeds benefit from natural cold stratification over winter before spring germination.

directions: A rare and beautiful native coneflower endemic to the wet longleaf pine flatwoods and savannas of Alabama, Georgia, and Florida's panhandle, producing bright yellow ray flowers surrounding a dark-brown, dome-shaped disk on tall, branching stems from July through October. Closely related to Rudbeckia laciniata but restricted to wet, acidic savanna habitats, it is an important component of longleaf pine savanna restoration plantings. Grow in moist to wet, acidic, sandy soil in full sun for best flowering; it tolerates periodic inundation but declines in dry or calcareous soils. An excellent and underused pollinator plant for Gulf Coast gardens and rain garden borders in zones 7-9.
sources: Center for Plant Conservation: Rudbeckia mohrii

planting.note: Surface-sow on moist soil at or just after last frost; do not cover seeds, as light aids germination.

directions: Slender, freely branching native annual or short-lived perennial smothered in masses of small white to pale lavender daisy-like flowers with yellow centers from late summer through October. Native to moist, open, disturbed habitats, ditches, and pond margins throughout the southeastern US, it is much more delicate in appearance than the larger Boltonia asteroides. Surface-sow on moist soil in full sun after last frost; plants naturalize readily by self-seeding in consistently moist sites. An excellent fine-textured filler for the back of rain garden borders and native wetland restorations where consistent moisture is available.
sources: Flora of North America: Boltonia diffusa
